question: A patient is undergoing arthroscopic repair with sutures passed through ligament bundles using a suture passer. The surgeon felt resistance while advancing sutures and obtained this intraoperative arthroscopic image. Based on the clinical scenario and the image, which complication is most likely?

options.A: Transection of a previously passed suture

ground_truth_reasoning:
First, I examine the arthroscopic image for relevant findings. I note a suture-passing instrument engaged in the ligament tissue, a previously placed suture lying posterior to the instrument (marked with an asterisk), and a short, irregular/blunt-appearing end of suture material immediately adjacent to the instrument tip (arrow). The surgeon reported feeling resistance while advancing sutures, which is an important clinical detail to relate to what I see.

Next, I relate these observations to the question, which asks which complication is most likely given the intraoperative image and the resistance encountered. A short, truncated suture end right next to the working tip is most consistent with a previously passed suture having been cut or transected when the passer was advanced against it. The proximity of the instrument to the previously placed suture and the visual appearance of a disrupted suture support this interpretation.

Then I consider the other options in light of the image. A fractured suture-passer tip would be suggested by visible metal fragmentation or a missing/abnormal instrument tip, but the passer tip here appears intact and whole. Iatrogenic articular cartilage injury would show focal cartilage abrasion or gouging on the visible surfaces, which is not apparent in this view. Intra-articular infection would not present acutely with the described mechanical resistance nor with the focal visual finding of a transected suture.

Finally, tying the image and the reported resistance together: the visual finding of a truncated suture end adjacent to the instrument, combined with the intraoperative sensation of resistance while advancing another suture, most directly indicates transection of a previously passed suture.
caption: (A) Arthroscopic view of a right knee, viewed from the anterolateral portal with the patient supine and the knee in 90° flexion. The anterior cruciate ligament is shown with a type I avulsion tear (asterisk) and an intact distal and middle part of the ligament with excellent tissue quality and vascularity (arrowhead). (B) Arthroscopic view of a right knee, viewed from the anterolateral portal with the patient supine and the knee in 90° flexion. A suture passer (arrowhead) is used to pass a No. 2 FiberWire suture through the anteromedial bundle. The suture is passed in an alternating, interlocking Bunnell-type pattern and advancing proximally when compared with the previous stitches (arrow). In the left top corner of the image, the No. 2 TigerWire sutures of the posterolateral bundle are seen (asterisk), because these were performed first in this patient.
sub_caption: (B) Arthroscopic view of a right knee, viewed from the anterolateral portal with the patient supine and the knee in 90° flexion. A suture passer (arrowhead) is used to pass a No. 2 FiberWire suture through the anteromedial bundle. The suture is passed in an alternating, interlocking Bunnell-type pattern and advancing proximally when compared with the previous stitches (arrow). In the left top corner of the image, the No. 2 TigerWire sutures of the posterolateral bundle are seen (asterisk), because these were performed first in this patient.
image_context: The patient is placed in the supine position, and the operative leg is prepped and draped as for standard knee arthroscopy. First, anteromedial and anterolateral portals are created, and a malleable Passport cannula (Arthrex, Naples, FL) is placed in the anteromedial portal for suture passage and management. A general inspection of the knee is performed for tear type and tissue quality, assessing the possibility of primary repair surgery (<xref rid="gr1" ref-type="fig">Fig 1</xref>A).A).7Fig 1(A) Arthroscopic view of a right knee, viewed from the anterolateral portal with the patient supine and the knee in 90° flexion. The anterior cruciate ligament is shown with a type I avulsion tear (asterisk) and an intact distal and middle part of the ligament with excellent tissue quality and vascularity (arrowhead). (B) Arthroscopic view of a right knee, viewed from the anterolateral portal with the patient supine and the knee in 90° flexion. A suture passer (arrowhead) is used to pass a No. 2 FiberWire suture through the anteromedial bundle. The suture is passed in an alternating, interlocking Bunnell-type pattern and advancing proximally when compared with the previous stitches (arrow). In the left top corner of the image, the No. 2 TigerWire sutures of the posterolateral bundle are seen (asterisk), because these were performed first in this patient. The first part of this surgical technique is similar to arthroscopic primary repair without suture augmentation.9 First, the anteromedial and posterolateral bundles of the ACL are identified, and the suturing is commenced. The sutures are passed through the anteromedial bundle using the Scorpion Suture Passer (Arthrex) with a No. 2 FiberWire suture (Arthrex) from the intact distal end in an alternating, interlocking Bunnell-type pattern toward the avulsed proximal end (<xref rid="gr1" ref-type="fig">Fig 1</xref>B). Three to four passes can usually be made before the final pass exits the proximal end toward the femur. Similarly, the suturing of the posterolateral bundle is performed using a No. 2 TigerWire suture (Arthrex). It should be noted that the suture-passing device should be repositioned if great resistance is experienced, because this can lead to transection of the previously passed suture.B). Three to four passes can usually be made before the final pass exits the proximal end toward the femur. Similarly, the suturing of the posterolateral bundle is performed using a No. 2 TigerWire suture (Arthrex). It should be noted that the suture-passing device should be repositioned if great resistance is experienced, because this can lead to transection of the previously passed suture.

A suture passer (arrowhead) is used to pass a No. 2 FiberWire suture through the anteromedial bundle. In the left top corner of the image, the No. 2 TigerWire sutures of the posterolateral bundle are seen (asterisk), because these were performed first in this patient. The first part of this surgical technique is similar to arthroscopic primary repair without suture augmentation.9 First, the anteromedial and posterolateral bundles of the ACL are identified, and the suturing is commenced. The sutures are passed through the anteromedial bundle using the Scorpion Suture Passer (Arthrex) with a No. 2 FiberWire suture (Arthrex) from the intact distal end in an alternating, interlocking Bunnell-type pattern toward the avulsed proximal end. Three to four passes can usually be made before the final pass exits the proximal end toward the femur. Similarly, the suturing of the posterolateral bundle is performed using a No. 2 TigerWire suture (Arthrex). It should be noted that the suture-passing device should be repositioned if great resistance is experienced, because this can lead to transection of the previously passed suture.

knowledge.topic: advancing suture passer against existing suture
knowledge.claim_text: Advancing a suture passer against an existing suture can transect (cut) the existing suture.
knowledge.presence_question: Does the model's response discuss advancing a suture passer against an existing suture?
knowledge.correctness_question: Does the model's response correctly state that advancing a suture passer against an existing suture can transect (cut) the existing suture?

knowledge.claim_text: A transected (cut) suture typically appears on arthroscopy as a short, irregular, blunt-appearing truncated end.
knowledge.presence_question: Does the model's response discuss the arthroscopic appearance of a transected suture?
knowledge.correctness_question: Does the model's response correctly state that a transected (cut) suture typically appears on arthroscopy as a short, irregular, blunt-appearing truncated end?

knowledge.claim_text: Intraoperative resistance while advancing a suture typically indicates contact with existing suture material and may signal a risk of suture transection.
knowledge.presence_question: Does the model's response discuss intraoperative resistance while advancing sutures?
knowledge.correctness_question: Does the model's response correctly state that intraoperative resistance while advancing a suture typically indicates contact with existing suture material and may signal a risk of suture transection?

reasoning.claim_text: The visual finding of a short, irregular, blunt-appearing truncated suture end adjacent to the instrument tip supports that the previously placed suture was transected in this case.
reasoning.presence_question: Does the model's response make an inferential link about Truncated suture suggests transection?
reasoning.correctness_question: Does the model's response correctly conclude that The visual finding of a short, irregular, blunt-appearing truncated suture end adjacent to the instrument tip supports that the previously placed suture was transected in this case.?

reasoning.claim_text: The intraoperative sensation of resistance while advancing sutures supports that the instrument contacted existing suture material and that this contact could have contributed to suture transection in this case.
reasoning.presence_question: Does the model's response make an inferential link about Resistance indicates instrument-suture contact?
reasoning.correctness_question: Does the model's response correctly conclude that The intraoperative sensation of resistance while advancing sutures supports that the instrument contacted existing suture material and that this contact could have contributed to suture transection in this case.?

reasoning.topic: Combined findings consistent with suture transection
reasoning.claim_text: The combination of the visual finding of a truncated suture end adjacent to the passer and the reported resistance while advancing sutures, together with the mechanism that advancing a passer against an existing suture can transect it, is consistent with a previously passed suture being transected in this case.
